title: 2025/12/01 PP (Part 1)
displayTitle: 2025-12-01 - Primary Production Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of Landcorp Farming Limited (Pāmu Farms) [Part 1]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of Landcorp Farming Limited (Pāmu Farms)
description: Monday 1 December | Primary Production Committee 2024/25 Annual review of Landcorp Farming Limited (Pāmu Farms) Landcorp Farming Limited John Rae, Chair Mark Leslie, Chief Executive Officer Will Burrett, Chief Operating Officer Andrew Sliper, Chief Investment Officer
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of Landcorp Farming Limited (Pāmu Farms). Landcorp Farming Limited. John Rae, Chair

chunks.text: Last story because don't get as many opportunities that we'd like to tell Palmer Lanco story. Just by way of introduction, I've been given very strong message to keep it short. So I will just, the key themes that I'd like to leave you with from, from my perspective, um, firstly is around financial performance. It is, it is numbers one, two, and three.

In our focus, we have to be, we have to earn our right to do other things. And, and that's based around operational excellence and financial performance. And we take that really seriously. Uh, this year we delivered in a net operating profit or last year, net operating profit of $49 million. And our forecast for 2026 is between 80 and $90 million.

Um, we consider that a significant turnaround. Not all of that is based around protein prices and significant, the not a significant increase between last year and this year. But our operational performance underpins that. Um, uh, our performance on farm livestock and land management has been exemplary in our view.

Uh, we've done managed to achieve emissions reductions, freshwater improvements. We continue with that sort of license top rate stuff that we think is really important work in the East coast, try to deal and help with significant dislocation problems with changing climate. Uh, on the East Coast where we have a significant investment, uh, we continue to invest in value beyond pure profit.

Still play a role around, uh, private sector leadership and innovation. Um, but it's all predicated now on value accretion. So we won't do anything for the sake of doing the right thing, but we do it where there's a, a net benefit to our p and l, uh, we'll at least no cost to our p and l, uh, and where there's a, a social benefit as well.

And that's a bit of a, uh, easing off of where we would've been in previous years. But, uh, we think that's the appropriate balance between economic outputs and, uh, and doing the right thing at the system level. We have, um, some, um, interesting and challenging conversations around, uh, ownership changes, as you will have heard in the press.

Um, and we spend a lot of time focusing on our long-term relationships with Iwi as a, as a repository for the Crowns, uh, treaty settlement program, uh, in farm in farmland particularly. Um, and we spend quite a bit of time positioning our portfolio in, in respect of what we would do for, for our farming systems if those farms went out of our system and into, into alternative ownership.

Uh, but that's just one of the limitations and realities of the portfolio that we have In terms of the ownership position, the crown, we tightly respect the crown has the right to make a decision, uh, around ownership or change of ownership. Uh, our job is to give a best sense of what that should look like, preserve the value that we believe that farming brings to the farming system, and we continue to prosecute that, um, and make sure that we fully understand what we might be giving away at the first instance if we,

uh, were not able, um, to retain this asset in the way. It's, um, so thanks very much for that, this opportunity. We look forward in the course of today to be able to fill in any gaps that you might have. Um, and, um, and, and just in closing, really it is important that across government, that the, the position of LandCorp and the system, at least for me, is really well understood.

It's a critical piece of the system, which I think we would miss if it was gone. And so we need to fight to understand it at the very least. And, and it's why committee such as this is so important. Thank you, uh, Joe, Thank you. Um, I've got lots of questions today, but um, before I do start with my, um, questions, I just want to, um, make mention of the fact that I visit quite a few of the, um, farms recently.

And I just wanna acknowledge the amazing staff that you have. They're always, um, really accommodating and, um, happy and willing to share anything and everything with me on those visits. And I just want, I think, to acknowledge that you've got a really great team out there. Um, you kind of just touched on it a wee bit there, but I'm interested to know, um, what it would mean for the organization if Palm were to divest.

Um, okay, I'll answer it. Um, in the best way I would can think of if, if the farms are, the, the, the advantages of having a portfolio at scale as we do is reasonably unique. There are pockets of scale around the country, but a broad canvas of multi-functional, multi-product farms across the country is something that we probably take for granted in my estimation.

I think that the, the ability for the system to get access to what works and what doesn't work is, um, is an opportunity we don't take advantage of particularly. Um, on the flip side of that coin there is, we will have to acknowledge there's a considerable amount of capital, um, tied up in it. So from where I sit, I've made it very clear that a ticket to the game for us is financial performance and operational excellence.

And without that, we do not earn the right to do anything else. And if we don't make it have a pass mark there, then we shouldn't be part of the part of the portfolio. If we earn that ticket to the game, then I think there's a, a broader conversation that the country needs to have about what role it might play in the future. And that's particularly around Deglobalize environment and all the things that are happening in this world, which mean that economic sovereignty and food security and all those things are things that we've never had

to consider in our other many years we've been, um, in this fabulous venue. So, so my job and for the, the team will, will do this work is to work out financial performance is necessary but not sufficient to keep us in the portfolio. And we're working on that second piece now. Thank you.

Thank you Steve. Um, on another line, I suppose a reflection I have on your statement about the financial stuff is that LandCorp also also uniquely brings an ability to get insight to the challenges in particular farming regions and farming systems and work on innovation in that space.

Most recently, we've had a very strong briefing on the Madagascar rag war problem where Palmer's insights were invaluable to us understanding the problem. I just want you to reflect on that's something beyond simply financial performance that's, um, the reliable authority within the active farming sector.

'cause you are farming who can help inform the government of what's going on in different systems in different regions. How can you reflect on that, please? Industry good? Yeah, look, I I think that, I mean, my observation is New Zealand's very good at thinking about the traditional orthodoxy until it's gone.

And then we say, I didn't realize we were, so, we got such valuable insights here, there, and, and everywhere. So I think that's why I've, I've asked for room to have a think about what it is that we might miss by not having institution doing what it's doing with, with commercial underpinned by commercial performance, but augmented by industry good as, as Susie, um, pointed out.

And, and then there's a whole lot of other stuff that comes to that democratization of our data with 112 farms. The experimentation, uh, with these bringing, uh, forestry and horticultures potential additionality to people's farming platform, people can actually see whether that works or not and why it might not work.

Um, this is getting started to get a long way away from my pay grade and I need to, we won and get the, the experts on it. But that would be my, at a board level, that that observation. Can I just point out, mark, nice to see you and I'm glad that you're not wearing your robe. Bless you. I, no, thank you so much.

I'll have a few questions for you. Good gentlemen. When, um, the chair is obliging, Uh, we're all, we're all glad of that. Um, can I just finish off on sec 'cause I think it is an important one that very much those areas where it does play into our wheelhouse where we have concerns, we'll absolutely step up into that.

'cause that's the Madagascar ragle one's a good example. We are seeing it early on. 'cause it's having a direct commercial impact on us as well. Mm-hmm. solid dollars per milk solid, and what you are operating at at dollars per kilo live weight to the works for your red meat sector. It's imminently due. And then we'll work through that regardless. We've got, we will absolutely make sure the right thing is done with that property.
